016package org.consensusj.analytics.util.collector;
017
018
019import java.util.Set;
020import java.util.function.BiConsumer;
021import java.util.function.BinaryOperator;
022import java.util.function.Function;
023import java.util.function.Supplier;
024import java.util.stream.Collector;
025
026/**
027 * Collector that collects the {@code n} objects containing the "largest slices" of an input stream of
028 * type {@code <E>}. Each object must have a numeric "slice size" of type {@code <N>} determined by a {@code sliceSizeExtractor}
029 * provided to the constructor. The result is a {@link LargestSliceList} containing the {@code n} largest slices
030 * ({@link LargestSliceList#getSliceList()}) and the sum of the slice sizes ({@link LargestSliceList#getTotalOther()}) of
031 * the filtered/excluded objects. The idea is the results can easily be converted to a Pie Chart.
032 * <p>
033 * The objects streamed must be unique, non-null, and a {@code sliceSizeExtractor} must be provided that
034 * will return a {@link Comparable} subclass of {@link Number}. The internal {@link LargestSliceAccumulator} is
035 * a mutable implementation of {@link LargestSliceList}
036 * <p>
037 * Note: Be careful to use only a single {@code sliceSizeExtractor} or you can end up with {@link LargestSliceList} objects
038 * with incompatible search orders (comparators).
039 * 
040 * @param <E> The type of objects
041 * @param <N> The subclass of {@link Number} to use, must be {@link Comparable}
042 */
043public class LargestSliceCollector<E, N extends Number & Comparable<? super N>>
044        implements Collector<E, LargestSliceAccumulator<E, N>, LargestSliceList<E, N>> {
045    private final int n;   // Maximum number to track
046    private final N zero;
047    private final Function<E, N> sliceSizeExtractor;
048    private final BinaryOperator<N> additionOperator;
049    
050    /**
051     * Construct a {@code LargestSliceCollector} to collect {@code n} objects of type {@code <E>}. The remaining
052     * constructor parameters provide support for the necessary internal calculations for types {@code <E>} and {@code <N>}.
053     *
054     * @param n Number of "slices" to collect
055     * @param sliceSizeExtractor A {@link Function} to return the slice size of an object
056     * @param zero The value of zero for type N
057     * @param additionOperator addition operator for type N
058     */
059    public LargestSliceCollector(int n, Function<E, N> sliceSizeExtractor, N zero, BinaryOperator<N> additionOperator) {
060        this.n = n;
061        this.sliceSizeExtractor = sliceSizeExtractor;
062        this.zero = zero;
063        this.additionOperator = additionOperator;
064    }
065
066    @Override
067    public Supplier<LargestSliceAccumulator<E, N>> supplier() {
068        return () -> new LargestSliceAccumulator<>(n, sliceSizeExtractor, zero, additionOperator);
069    }
070
071    @Override
072    public BiConsumer<LargestSliceAccumulator<E, N>, E> accumulator() {
073        return LargestSliceAccumulator::accumulate;
074    }
075
076    @Override
077    public BinaryOperator<LargestSliceAccumulator<E, N>> combiner() {
078        return LargestSliceAccumulator::combine;
079    }
080
081
082    /**
083     * Return a finisher function.
084     *
085     * @return A finisher function
086     */
087    @Override
088    public Function<LargestSliceAccumulator<E, N>, LargestSliceList<E, N>> finisher() {
089        return (accum) -> new LargestSliceListImpl<>(accum.getSortedSliceList(), accum.getTotalOther());
090    }
091
092    /**
093     * Note that our accumulator won't work correctly <b>or</b> efficiently if a single accumulator
094     * is used from multiple threads. It works with parallel streams, but not with a CONCURRENT collector.
095     * See: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22350288/parallel-streams-collectors-and-thread-safety
096     * 
097     * @return The characteristics of this collector.
098     */
099    @Override
100    public Set<Characteristics> characteristics() {
101        return Set.of(Characteristics.UNORDERED);
102    }
103}
